text: Fundamental aspects of Fischer-Tropsch (FT) synthesis have been under debate for almost a century. Most publications focus on the molecular level such as the structure of the active sites and the catalytic reaction network for Co- and Fe-based catalysts. During the last 15 years, a surface-science- and molecular-modeling-based bottom-up approach has made significant progress toward a molecular understanding of FT. This feature topical review highlights progress on the structural models of Co particles and on the effect of realistic high-pressure surface conditions, which has become possible through recent surface science investigations in combination with density functional theory studies. These provide the basis for microkinetic modeling and mechanistic studies of Co-based Fischer-Tropsch synthesis that seem to have approached a consensus on the active site structure and reaction mechanism. The understanding of the structural changes of Fe-based catalysts during FT synthesis at the surface level is complicated by dynamic phase changes, which are extremely difficult to quantify. Recent experimental strategies are expected to facilitate the understanding of the high degree of complexity involved. In addition, recent experimental and density functional theory investigations have provided understanding of the overall reaction mechanism for Fe-based catalysts. However, a clear structural identification of the active sites, which is crucial to get a molecular understanding of the catalytic mechanism, has not yet been achieved. Most of the catalysts used for FT synthesis are formed in situ from iron precursors; thus, it is also interesting to explore alternative routes to FT by direct CO<sub>2</sub> hydrogenation to form long hydrocarbons.
